data: A marble funerary plaque, broken into multiple fragments and currently reconnected with plaster filling the voids. Orsi records 7 fragments (in both the Taccuini and the NSA), although the inventory entries both note only 6 fragments, and the transcription under inv.50779 (1948) misses the final letters lower right of the 7th fragment. The 7th fragment was not with the stone when last inspected (2015). It is unclear from Orsi's drawing whether the lower left corner was fully preserved in the 7th fragment originally or not, and therefore as it is currently preserved, at least one line is lost at the bottom. The plaque is thicker at the top than at the bottom.
Well-spaced lines filling the width of the available stone, word-breaks do not observe line-end.

Somewhat irregular deep-cut letters with pronounced terminations and occasionally extravagant serifs/flourishes (e.g. on X or E), and a varying tendency to the later narrow elongated style (e.g. TFL in line 5), and M sometimes with the upper strokes extended well above the join. A consistently lacks a cross-bar; E generally has equal length strokes, but with the top stroke often extended upwards; F, tall, narrow, and upward pointing; M sometimes with extended apices, sometimes without (all four strokes full length); P is closed; R sometimes open, with extended tail; X frequently over extended upper right. No interpuncts.
mid-2nd century to mid-4th century CE
Photo J. Prag (2015), Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014

An outstanding collection of archaeological material. The collection ranges from Siracusa and the surrounding territory (Megara Hyblaia, Akrai, Kasmenai etc) to encompass all of eastern Sicily, reaching as far as Gela and Agrigento. The ground floor covers prehistory through to the end of the Classical period. Upstairs includes Hellenistic and Roman Syracuse and a display of material from the catacombs. A gallery displaying material from Hellenistic eastern Sicily is in preparation.

Accademia nazionale dei Lincei, Istituto nazionale di archeologia e storia dell’arte (Italy), e Reale Accademia d’Italia, «Notizie degli scavi di antichità», Notizie degli Scavi di Antichità, 1876, http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1646037
I Taccuini. I. Riproduzione anastatica e trascrizione dei Taccuini 1-4

Paolo Orsi, I Taccuini. I. Riproduzione anastatica e trascrizione dei Taccuini 1-4, a c. di Gioconda Lamagna e Giuseppina Monterosso, Accademia Nazionale dei Lincei. Monumenti Antichi. Serie Miscellanea 20 (Rome: Giorgio Bretschneider Editore, 2018)
Ephemeris epigraphica. Corporis inscriptionum Latinarum supplementum, edita iussu Instituti archaeologici Romani
W. Henzen and K. F. W. Zangemeister, Ephemeris Epigraphica. Corporis Inscriptionum Latinarum Supplementum, Edita Iussu Instituti Archaeologici Romani, 9 vols (Berlin: G. Reimerum, 1872)

l.4 reg., orig. ux xor | l.7 reg., orig. vix xit

Facsimile Images

Apparatus criticus

  • line.6: The top of the B is visible above the break. The final two Es on this line appear either partially or entirely to lack the lowermost stroke.

English translation

Jonathan Prag

To the shades of the departed. This memorial of Cesernius Marcelus his wife and sons set up for a well deserving man, who lived 43 (?) years ...

commentary

The fragment recorded by Orsi from the lower left recorded an initial X to the numeral and three letters in a 10th line below, which are not easily resolved in a common abbreviation but likely to pertain to the erection of the memorial by Cesernius Marcelus' family. The position of the X at the start of line 9, with at least a partial vacat of one character implied before it in the drawing by Orsi leaves it slightly uncertain exactly what the total age in years recorded was, with a minimum of 33 and a maximum of 53, but 43 seeming the most likely from the spacing on the fragments preserved. The repetition of X across line-break in both uxor and vixit is notable.

bibliography

  • VIII 688 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10002649
  • Orsi NSA 1889 385 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10001392
  • Orsi 2018 Tacc. 1, p. 41 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10002617

Somewhat irregular deep-cut letters with pronounced terminations and occasionally extravagant serifs/flourishes (e.g. on X or E), and a varying tendency to the later narrow elongated style (e.g. TFL in line 5), and M sometimes with the upper strokes extended well above the join. A consistently lacks a cross-bar; E generally has equal length strokes, but with the top stroke often extended upwards; F, tall, narrow, and upward pointing; M sometimes with extended apices, sometimes without (all four strokes full length); P is closed; R sometimes open, with extended tail; X frequently over extended upper right. No interpuncts.

Dis Manibus memoriam Ce serni Marce li posuit ux xoruxor sua et fili sui bene me renti qui vix xitvixit annis XXXIII SFV
The top of the B is visible above the break. The final two Es on this line appear either partially or entirely to lack the lowermost stroke.

To the shades of the departed. This memorial of Cesernius Marcelus his wife and sons set up for a well deserving man, who lived 43 (?) years ...

The fragment recorded by Orsi from the lower left recorded an initial X to the numeral and three letters in a 10th line below, which are not easily resolved in a common abbreviation but likely to pertain to the erection of the memorial by Cesernius Marcelus' family. The position of the X at the start of line 9, with at least a partial vacat of one character implied before it in the drawing by Orsi leaves it slightly uncertain exactly what the total age in years recorded was, with a minimum of 33 and a maximum of 53, but 43 seeming the most likely from the spacing on the fragments preserved. The repetition of X across line-break in both uxor and vixit is notable.
